speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four on April 8, 1996, with survivor Hy Abrams. OK. So tell me about your life here in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, when I came to the United States, I stood by my uncle. My uncle had three kids, three daughters. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In the second-- about two weeks later, I told my uncle I want to work. He says, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said no, I want to work. And I found a job, I started to work. And I found a job in a-- in a furniture factory. It wasn't bad. I didn't make a fortune, but it was OK. I was single. I didn't have much-- too many expenses. And let me tell about in 1952, I wanted to build a family. I got married. And we used to live in the Bronx. We had a daughter in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name was Irene.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Richards. And in 1953, we had a daughter. Her name is Cynthia. By the way, her name, Yiddish name Channa [? Etia ?] after my mother. And I started to work-- I started to work in a-- in a kitchen factory-- in a kitchen cabinet factory, basically, as a woodworker. And-- I didn't have it very easy, because sometimes I had to do-- I had to do two jobs. But still and all, I was glad. I was glad with my work, I was glad that I have a family, that I'm building a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So basically up to 1967, 1967 I came home. We went out-- we went out with my wife. We had-- my wife and her family, we had dinner outside, and we came home. And I was sitting down watching television. My sister-in-law, my wife's brother's wife calls, and she says, I would like to talk to Irene. I say, wait a minute, I'm going to get her. I went in the bedroom. I told her she's in the bedroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- and I saw she's not in the bedroom, then I see there is a light in the bathroom. I knock on to the door. Nobody answers. I knock again, nobody answers. I-- I didn't feel like open up the door. So my daughter saw, you know, that I'm knocking, nobody answered. So she tried to open up the door. She couldn't open the door to open, because unfortunately my wife Irene was dead. Found out that she had a massive stroke. That was in 1967, on Labor Day. So my daughter was barely 14 years old that time. So I had to-- I had to be a father and a mother to my daughter. It's not one of the most easiest things to have a 14-year-old girl, special for a father to-- to tell her what life is all about. But thanks God, I have a very understanding daughter, and a very good daughter. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 1971, my daughter tells me, dad, I'm going out. I'm going to be 18 years old. I don't want you should stay alone. You shouldn't be alone. Why don't you look for somebody? Till then, I didn't-- I didn't bother to look for anything or anything, because I wanted my daughter to grow normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1971, a cousin of mine introduced me to Fay, to Faga. This is my wife. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We both went through. We understood each other, what life is all about. Her husband passed away. She had two daughters. So I started to see her, and we fell in love, and we got married. And thanks God, I got married to her. She's a wonderful wife. The-- the children are wonderful. We have one family. It's not like this is my children, or this is your children, and so on. Thanks God we have now great grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think Hitler couldn't destroy everything after all, even as hard as he tried. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It's-- it's Pesach now, and we had a beautiful seder. Our grandchildren were with us. Our great grandchildren. It was such a pleasure when we were singing [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Our great grandson is two years old. He was jumping around and singing [NON-ENGLISH]. Even his sister, she's eight months old. But she saw all that. She was jumping for joy herself. Now this is the most-- most beautiful pleasure what a great grandfather, a grandfather, a father could have.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, is there a special message you'd like to give to anybody viewing this video?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is this here. It's 50 years, 51 years after the war end. I'm going to be a optimist, like I said. In the next 50 years, I don't think anybody's going to be alive that went through it. It's up to-- to people, to schools to educate people, to show them that what hate-- what hate could bring.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there is only-- only also another message I want to-- I want to bring out. There's a lot of people that say, forgive and forget. I say, I could forgive what they did to me, but I cannot forgive what they did to my sisters, or to my parents. It's not up to me to forgive. It's up to them to forgive. Forget? No. You have to remember. You cannot forget or think like that. You should always remember what they did and why they did it. This is my message. Well, thank you for sharing your story with us. And we're going to look at photographs now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, on the right is my grandfather on my mother's side. His name was Yitzchok [? Leib ?] Fox. On the left is my grandmother, his wife, on my mother's side. It's the mother of my mother. Her name was Miriam, Miriam Fox.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say this picture was taken approximately, I would say 19-- 1928, '30.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, we're starting from the right. On the right is my grandfather, Yitzchok [? Leib. ?] Sitting next to him is my mother, my mother Channa Etia. Holding is my sister Chia. On her left side is a cousin of mine. In the front of my cousin is another cousin, Channa. On the left side is my aunt, [INAUDIBLE] Malka. She's holding her son by the name of Moshe. This picture was taken approximately, I would say in 1924 or '23.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Novoselytsia. In this picture is my mother, [INAUDIBLE] and my father. I think this picture was taken before they got married. Probably that was in 1920. This picture is from school in Novoselytsia. If you look at the picture, there is one fellow, he's on top of the picture, he's the tallest of them all. His name is [? Yitzhak ?]. He's in Israel. He's one-- one of my best friends. Any time I'm in Israel, I go to visit him. I got this picture-- this picture was saved somebody that lives in Budapest. And she has a sister in Israel. And she send that picture to her sister in Israel, and she gave me a duplicate of that picture. This is how I got that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture approximately was taken, I would say in 19-- oh, let me see. '24? Around 19-- 1932 in Novoselytsia. And what is [? Yitzhak ?]'s last name? His last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yitzhak ?] Binder.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Chia. She was born in 1922. This picture I would say was taken in 1943. This is-- this is the sister what I heard her last word when she said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Unfortunately, she was embedded in Bergen-Belsen. After the war, they took her to Sweden. She was very sick. She had typhus from the war. She died in Sweden, in Malmo. [NON-ENGLISH] in 1945. I was-- I was at the cemetery, and I have seen her-- her grave. Matter of fact, I even put a stone monument on her-- on her grave. That's the only one what have a monument on from all the victims.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. That's a good picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1945, in Novoselytsia. Those were all that remained alive from the war, all the young ones. If-- if you want to look for me, I'm going to give you a little time. But in the meantime, I'm gonna tell you if you look at the center line, the third from right, this is the girl what I left from Novoselytsia. And this is the girl what she was molested. If you didn't find where I am, I'm the second from the left, in-- in the middle row. Extreme left was my cousin. Like I said, this was taken in 1945. You could see that some of them still have German caps. I myself down there, I had a German jacket on. I didn't have anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Novoselytsia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Novoselytsia, OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, years ago I used to be but a handsome fellow. I know you recognized it's me. That picture was taken in Admont in 1947. I was a little younger, and I-- the way you can see, I still had hair. But it's OK. I'm alive, and I'm happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was made in Malmo, Sweden. When the Jewish community made the [NON-ENGLISH] over there, there's a lot of young people in that cemetery. So they made-- they-- they did the best what they could as far as spelling of the name is concerned. Now, we are gonna-- I'm gonna show later on after this picture, I'm gonna show the one-- the real spelling what we made, when we put up the [NON-ENGLISH] for my sister. This is my sister Chia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is-- is from my sister. Of course I changed the other [NON-ENGLISH]. And I put her real name, what the real name is supposed to be spelled, and I put it also in Yiddish. Like you say [NON-ENGLISH]. It says over there. This is the right spelling. And I could say one thing, that the community in Malmo did a excellent job to preserve all those-- the [NON-ENGLISH] for all the young people what are buried there in Malmo. I thank for the community. This book made by my niece [? Hagit, ?] my brother's daughter. When she finished high school, she had to go in-- in college. But she made this book. The book-- the book's name is [NON-ENGLISH]. She describes the community down there, from all the little towns and cities in [NON-ENGLISH] and the Carpathian. What-- what it was, how many people were there, what they did, and how, unfortunately, they got destroyed. So this is the book, and I thank for my-- for my niece for her wonderful work for making that book.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [? Hagit ?] Abrahamovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a happy ending for the story. From the right is my wife Fay. Next to her in the back is my daughter Cindy. On her right is my-- my granddaughter, Michelle. Next to her in the center is our grandson, David. Next to David in the back is our daughter, Carol. Next to Carol is Barbara. In the center is Scott. And of course, on the left side is handsome me. This picture was taken at Scott's bar mitzvah. That's the front little fella, but he's tall now.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a good one. I'm trying to think, apparently-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: No it's on. How old is Scott now, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] That was-- that was taken in 1986.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
